entry.title: Active Learning
entry.content:
Overview of Active Learning

  • Inspirated Scenario
    To classify a large amount of unlabeled data, but there is no labeled data available to train the classifier, and due to time / money cost constraints, it is difficult to label a large number of data sets for training the classifier.

  • The key hypothesis
    Whether the learning algorithm is allowed to choose the data from which it learns—to be “curious,” if you will—it will perform better with less training.

  • Goal
    Reduce labeling costs.

  • Sample Information - Entropy
    Before introducing the concept of active learning, we can first talk about sample information.
    Sample information can simply means: the information that each sample from training dataset brings to the training model is different. That is, each sample has a large or small different contribution to training model.

    And we can quantify this information by Entropy. Normally the higher entropy, the larger information will be.

  • Definition of Active Learning
    In some cases, the unlabeled data is quite abundant and the labeled data is quite rare, and the cost of manually labeling the data is quite high (both time and money). In this case, we can let the learning algorithm proactively propose which data should be labeled, and then we can send these data for labeling by experts, and finally add these new labeled data to the training dataset to train the algorithm. This process is called Active Learning.

Active learning VS semi-supervised learning

Many people think that active learning also belongs to semi-supervised learning, but in fact it is different. Semi-supervised learning, transductive learning and active learning are all learning techniques that use unlabeled data, but the principal idea behind them is different.

As mentioned above, the “active” of active learning refers to be initiative to make a labeling request, that is to say, an external entity (usually a person in the relevant field) that can label the data is required, which means, active learning is an interactive process.

Semi-supervised learning specifically refers to only use unlabeled data based on the learning algorithm self without manual intervention.

Active learning Scenarios

  • Membership query synthesis, i.e., a generated sample is sent to an oracle for labeling.
    This is a big term which simply means that the learner generates/constructs an instance (from some underlying natural distribution). For example, if the data is pictures of digits, the learner would create an image that is similar to a digit (it might be rotated or some piece of the digit excluded) and this created image is sent to the oracle to label.

  • Stream-Based selective sampling, i.e, each sample is considered separately - in our case for lable-querying or rejection. Similarly to online-learning, the data is not saved, there are no assumptions on data distribution, and therefore it is adaptive to change.

  • Pool-Based sampling, i.e., sampled are chosen from a pool of unlabeled data for the purpose of labeling. In this tutorial we use the third scenario. This setting assumes that there is a large pool of unlabelled data, as with the stream-based selective sampling. Instances are then drawn from the pool according to some informativeness measure. This measure is applied to all instances in the pool (or some subset if the pool is very large) and then the most informative instance(s) are selected. This is the most common scenario in the active learning community.


Composition and stages for Active learning:

  • Composition
    Active learning algorithms can be modeled by following five components:

    Where $C$ is a classifier or a group of classifiers; $𝐿$ is a set of labeled training samples; $𝑄$ is a query function or strategy used to query samples with large amounts of information in unlabeled samples; $𝑈$ is the entire unlabeled sample set; $𝑆$ is a expert who can label unlabeled samples.

  • Stages
    First stage: the initialization stage.
    A small part of the unlabeled samples is randomly selected and labeled by the experts as a training set to build an initial classifier model;
    The second stage: the cyclic query stage.
    In the unlabeled sample set $𝑈$, certain unlabeled samples are selected for labeling and added to the training sample set $𝐿$ according to a certain query standard $𝑄$, and the classifier is retrained until the training stop criteria is reached.



Concrete steps for Pool-Based sampling scenario

  1. Divide the data to a ‘pool’ and a test-set
  2. Select ‘k’ samples from the pool for the initial train-set and label them, the remaining data will be the validation-set
  3. Normalize all the sets
  4. Train the Model using the train-set, with balanced weights.
  5. Use the trained model with the validation-set, get probabilities per sample.
  6. Use the trained model with the test-set, get performance measures.
  7. Select ‘k’ most-informative samples based on per-sample-probabilities, i.e., those that the model was most uncertain about regarding their labelling.
  8. Move these ‘k’ samples from the validation set to the train-set and query their labels.
  9. Inverse normalization for all the data-sets
  10. Stop according to the stop criterion, otherwise go to 3.

Query / Selection Strategy

I will use the table below to explain the query strategies. This table shows two data points (instances) and the probabilities that each instance has each label.

  • Random selection: we just select ‘k’ random samples from the unlabeled dataset.

  • Least Confidence (LC): in this strategy, the learner selects the instance for which it has the least confidence in its most likely label. Looking at the table, the leaner is pretty confident about the label for d1, since it thinks it should be labelled A with probability 0.9, however, it is less sure about the label of d2 since its probabilities are more spread and it thinks that it should be labelled B with a probability of only 0.5. Thus, using least confidence, the learner would select d2 to query it’s actual label.

  • Margin Sampling: the shortcoming of the LC strategy, is that it only takes into consideration the most probable label and disregards the other label probabilities. The margin sampling strategy seeks to overcome this disadvantage by selecting the instance that has the smallest difference between the first and second most probable labels. Looking at d1, the difference between its first and second most probable labels is 0.81 (0.9 - 0.09) and for d2 it is 0.2 (0.5 - 0.3). Hence, the leaner will select d2 again.

  • Entropy Sampling: in order to utilize all the possible label probabilities, we can also use a popular measure called entropy. The entropy formula is applied to each instance and the instance with the largest value is queried.

Stopping criteria

  • All unlabeled data is running out.

  • No money or access for further labeling.

  • Required to stop progress.

entry.title: Data Augmentation in NLP
entry.content:
Data Augmentation is an effective way to expand the size of data samples. The larger the data size and the higher the quality we can get. And the model can have better generalization ability.
Unlike computer vision using images for data augmentation, text data augmentation in NLP is very rare, because some simple operations on the image, such as rotating the image or converting it to grayscale, do not change its semantics, while text data is not.


Approaches for Data Augmentation in NLP

Easy Data Augmentation，EDA

  • Synonym Replacement
    Randomly select n words that are not in the stop word set from the sentence, and randomly select their synonyms to replace them;

  • Random Insertion
    Randomly find a word in the sentence that does not belong to the stop word set, find its random synonyms, and insert the synonyms into a random position in the sentence. Repeat n times;

  • Random Swap
    Randomly choose two words in the sentence and swap their positions. Repeat n times;

  • Random Deletion
    Randomly remove each word in the sentence with the probability p

Lexical Substitution

  • Words dictionary-based substitution
    In this technique, we take a random word from the sentence and replace it with its synonym using a Thesaurus (Words dictionary). For example, we could use the WordNet database for English to look up the synonyms and then perform the replacement. It is a manually curated database with relations between words.

  • Word-Embeddings Substitution
    In this approach, we take pre-trained word embeddings such as Word2Vec, GloVe, FastText, Sent2Vec, and use the nearest neighbor words in the embedding space as the replacement for some word in the sentence.

    It’s easy to use packages like Gensim to access pre-trained word vectors and get the nearest neighbors. For example, here we find the synonyms for the word ‘awesome’ using word vectors trained on tweets.

    You will get back the 5 most similar words along with the cosine similarities.

  • Masked Language Model
    Transformer models such as BERT, ROBERTA and ALBERT have been trained on a large amount of text using a pretext task called “Masked Language Modeling” where the model has to predict masked words based on the context.
    This can be used to augment some text. For example, we could use a pre-trained BERT model, mask some parts of the text and ask the BERT model to predict the token for the mask.

    hus, we can generate variations of a text using the mask predictions. Compared to previous approaches, the generated text is more grammatically coherent as the model takes context into account when making predictions.

    This is easy to implement with open-source libraries such as transformers by Hugging Face. You can set the token you want to replace with and generate predictions.

  • TF-IDF based word replacement
    The basic idea is that words that have low TF-IDF scores are uninformative and thus can be replaced without affecting the ground-truth labels of the sentence.
    The words that replaces the original word are chosen by calculating TF-IDF scores of words over the whole document and taking the lowest ones.

Back Translation

In this approach, we leverage machine translation to paraphrase a text while retraining the meaning.

The back-translation process is as follows:

  • Take some sentence (e.g. in English) and translate to another Language e.g. French
  • Translate the French sentence back into English sentence
  • Check if the new sentence is different from our original sentence. If it is, then we use this new sentence as an augmented version of the original text.

You can also run back-translation using different languages at once to generate more variations. As shown below, we translate an English sentence to a target language and back again to English for three target languages: French, Mandarin and Italian.

Text Surface Transformation

These are simple pattern matching transformations applied using regex and was introduced by Claude Coulombe in his paper.

In the paper, he gives an example of transforming verbal forms from contraction to expansion and vice versa. We can generate augmented texts by applying this.

Since the transformation should not change the meaning of the sentence, we can see that this can fail in case of expanding ambiguous verbal forms like:

Random Noise Injection

The idea of these methods is to inject noise in the text so that the model trained is robust to perturbations.

  • Spelling error injection
    In this method, we add spelling errors to some random word in the sentence. These spelling errors can be added programmatically or using a mapping of common spelling errors such as this list for English.

  • QWERTY Keyboard Error Injection

  • Unigram Noising

  • Blank Noising
    The idea is to replace some random word with a placeholder token. The paper uses “_” as the placeholder token.

  • Sentence Shuffling
    This is a naive technique where we shuffle sentences present in a training text to create an augmented version.

Instance Crossover Augmentation

This technique was inspired by the chromosome crossover operation that happens in genetics.
In the method, a tweet are divided into two halves and two random tweets of the same polarity(i.e. positive/negative) have their halves swapped. The hypothesis is that even though the result will be ungrammatical and semantically unsound, the new text will still preserve the sentiment.

Syntax-tree Manipulation

The idea is to parse and generate the dependency tree of the original sentence, transform it using rules and generate a paraphrased sentence.
For example, one transformation that doesn’t change the meaning of the sentence is the transformation from active voice to the passive voice of sentence and vice versa.

MixUp for Text

Mixup is a simple yet effective image augmentation technique introduced by Zhang et al. in 2017. The idea is to combine two random images in a mini-batch in some proportion to generate synthetic examples for training.

  • wordMixup
    In this method, two random sentences in a mini-batch are taken and they are zero-padded to the same length. Then, their word embeddings are combined in some proportion. The resulting word embedding is passed to the usual flow for text classification. The cross-entropy loss is calculated for both the labels of the original text in the given proportion.
  • sentMixup
    In this method, two sentences are taken and they are zero-padded to the same length. Then, their word embeddings are passed through LSTM/CNN encoder and we take the last hidden state as sentence embedding. These embeddings are combined in a certain proportion and then passed to the final classification layer. The cross-entropy loss is calculated based on both the labels of original sentences in the given proportion.

Generative Methods

  • Conditional Pre-trained Language Models
    • Prepend the class label to each text in your training data

    • Finetune a large pre-trained language model(BERT/GPT2/BART) on this modified training data. For GPT2, the fine-tuning task is generation while for BERT, the goal would be masked token prediction.

    • Using the fine-tuned language model, new samples can be generated by using the class label and few initial words as the prompt for the model. The paper uses 3 initial words of each training text and also generates one synthetic example for each point in the training data.

Ref-1
Ref-2

entry.title: Stacking
entry.content:
What is Stack?

Using another different machine learning algorithm to combine the results of individual machine learners, we call it as Stacking ensemble.

By stacking method, we call call each individual learner as primary learner, the learner used for combination is called the secondary learner or meta-learner, and the data used for training by the secondary learner is called the secondary training set. The secondary training set is obtained by using the primary learner on the primary training set, which mean the secondary training set is the prediction result of the primary learner.

  • Process 1-3: to train individual learners - primary learners.
  • Process 5-9: to use the trained individual learner to obtain the predicted result, and this predicted result is used as the training set for the secondary learner.
  • Process 11: to train the secondary learner with the prediction result of the primary learner, and the get the final model we want.

Stacking Implement

The first method that comes to mind is:

  1. Use data set D to train primary learners h1, h2, h3…,
  2. Use these trained primary learners to make predictions on the data set D to obtain the secondary training set.
  3. Use the secondary training set to train the secondary learners.

But this method has big flaws. The model trained on the original data set D, and then the secondary training set obtained by using these models to predict on D has definitely good quality which will lead to overfitting.

Then we can have following method which avoid to overfitting.
The composition of the secondary training set is not directly predicted by the model on training set D. Instead, the cross-validation method is used to divide the training set D into k parts. For each part, use the remaining data set to train the model, and then predict the result of this part. Repeat above steps until each part is predicted. In this way, the overfitting problem will not happen. And the secondary test set was also constructed in the process of constructing the secondary training set,

For all of our primary trainers, we must repeat the above steps to construct the final secondary training set and secondary test set.

Note: For the 5-fold of each model in primary learners, each model must be trained and predicted 5 times of 5-fold. And the primary learners can be consist of several different models.

entry.title: GBDT - XGBoost
entry.content:
GBDT steps

AdaBoost V.S. GBDT

AdaBoost用错分数据点来识别问题,通过调整错分数据点的权重来改进模型. Gradient Boosting通过负梯度来识别问题，通过计算负梯度来改进模型.

XGB V.S. GBDT:

  1. XGB对loss函数做泰勒二次展开, GBDT做泰勒一次展开.
  2. XGB对loss函数添加了L2正则.
  3. XGB可自定义代价函数，只要可以一二阶可导.

entry.title: Boosting - Adaboost
entry.content:
What is Boosting?

The mechanism of Boosting algorithm aims to firstly train a weak learner 1 from the training set with initial weights, and update the weights of the training set according to the performance of the weak learner 1 on learning error rate, so that the previous weak learner 1 learns the training samples with a high error rate. Also the weight becomes higher which makes these sample points with high error rate get more attention in the later weak learner 2. Then train the weak learner 2 based on the training set after adjusting the weights, and repeat this process until the number of weak learners reaches the pre-specified number T, and finally these T weak learners are integrated through the set strategy (average, voting, etc) to obtain the final strong Learners.



What is Adaboost?

AdaBoost (Adaptive Boosting) is a very popular boosting technique that aims at combining multiple weak classifiers to build one strong classifier.

A single classifier may not be able to accurately predict the class of an object, but when we group multiple weak classifiers with each one progressively learning from the others’ wrongly classified objects, we can build one such strong model. The classifier mentioned here could be any of your basic classifiers, from Decision Trees (often the default) to Logistic Regression, etc.


Steps of Adaboost

Step 1: A weak classifier (e.g. a decision stump) is made on top of the training data based on the weighted samples. Here, the weights of each sample indicate how important it is to be correctly classified. Initially, for the first stump, we give all the samples equal weights.

Step 2: We create a decision stump for each variable and see how well each stump classifies samples to their target classes.

Step 3: More weight is assigned to the incorrectly classified samples so that they’re classified correctly in the next decision stump. Weight is also assigned to each classifier based on the accuracy of the classifier, which means high accuracy = high weight!

Step 4: Reiterate from Step 2 until all the data points have been correctly classified, or the maximum iteration level has been reached.

Two import coefficients

  • Weight coefficient $e_i$ for Training data set.
  • Weight coefficient $a_i$ Classifier.

Concrete calculation steps of Adaboost

entry.title: Graph Embedding - DeepWalk
entry.content:
Inspiration

We need approach for learning latent representations of vertices in a network. These latent representations encode social relations in a continuous vector space, which is easily exploited by statistical models. DeepWalk generalizes recent advancements in language modeling and unsupervised feature learning (or deep learning) from sequences of words to graphs.

The sparsity of a network representation is both a strength and a weakness. Sparsity enables the design of efficient discrete algorithms, but can make it harder to generalize in statistical learning.

We introduce deep learning (unsupervised feature learning) techniques, which have proven successful in natural language processing, into network analysis for the first time. We develop an algorithm (DeepWalk) that learns social representations of a graph’s vertices, by modeling a stream of short random walks. Social representations are latent features of the vertices that capture neighborhood similarity and community membership. These latent representations encode social relations in a continuous vector space with a relatively small number of dimensions. DeepWalk generalizes neural language models to process a special language composed of a set of randomly-generated walks. These neural language models have been used to capture the semantic and syntactic structure of human language, and even logical analogies.

Random walk

“Random Walk is a technique to extract sequences from a graph. We can use these sequences to train a skip-gram model to learn node embeddings.”

The goal of Random Walk is to discover neighborhoods in the network. To do so, we generate a fixed number (k) of random walks starting at each node. The length of each walk is pre-determined. Thus, when this stage is finished, we obtain k node sequences of length l. The gist of random walk generation is the following assumption:

  • “Adjacent nodes are similar and should have similar embeddings.”
    Based on this assumption, we will accept nodes that co-occur in a path as similar nodes. This means that the frequency of co-occurrence in random walks is an indicator of node similarity. Note that this is a reasonable assumption since edges, by design, mostly denote similar or interacting nodes in a network.

Let me illustrate how Random Walk works. Let’s consider the undirected graph below:

We will apply Random Walk on this graph and extract sequences of nodes from it. We will start from Node 1 and cover two edges in any direction:

From node 1, we could have gone to any connected node (node 3 or node 4). We randomly selected node 4. Now again from node 4, we have to randomly choose our way forward. We’ll go with node 5. Now we have a sequence of 3 nodes: [node 1 – node 4 – node 5].
Let’s generate another sequence, but this time from a different node:
Let’s select node 15 as the originating node. From nodes 5 and 6, we will randomly select node 6. Then from nodes 11 and 2, we select node 2. The new sequence is [node 15 – node 6 – node 2].

We will repeat this process for every node in the graph. This is how the Random Walk technique works.
Here the effect of k and l is important. The more k is increased, the more the network is explored since more random walks are produced. On the other hand, when l is increased, paths become longer and more distant nodes are accepted as similar nodes. This corresponds to relaxing the similarity constraint and can introduce noisy and misleading co-occurrences.

Language model - Skip-gram}

Skip-gram algorithm is a popular technique used to learn word embeddings. It was introduced by Mikolov et. al. in their well-known paper on word2vec. Given a corpus and a window size, Skip-gram aims to maximize the similarity of word embeddings’ of the words that occur in the same window. These windows are also called context in NLP. This idea is based on the assumption that:

  • “Words that occur in the same context, tend to have close meanings. Therefore, their embeddings should be close to each other as well.”

We’re going to train the neural network to do the following. Given a specific word in the middle of a sentence (the input word), look at the words nearby and pick one at random. The network is going to tell us the probability for every word in our vocabulary of being the “nearby word” that we chose.
The output probabilities are going to relate to how likely it is find each vocabulary word nearby our input word. For example, if you gave the trained network the input word “Soviet”, the output probabilities are going to be much higher for words like “Union” and “Russia” than for unrelated words like “watermelon” and “kangaroo”.
We’ll train the neural network to do this by feeding it word pairs found in our training documents. The below example shows some of the training samples (word pairs) we would take from the sentence “The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.” I’ve used a small window size of 2 just for the example. The word highlighted in blue is the input word.

Here we can see internal architecture of Skip-gram model. Skip-gram aims to predict the context word for a given target word.

As we can see w(t) is the target word or input given. There is one hidden layer which performs the dot product between the weight matrix and the input vector w(t). No activation function is used in the hidden layer. Now the result of the dot product at the hidden layer is passed to the output layer. Output layer computes the dot product between the output vector of the hidden layer and the weight matrix of the output layer. Then we apply the softmax activation function to compute the probability of words appearing to be in the context of w(t) at given context location.

To adapt Skip-gram to networks, we need to determine what context corresponds to in the network world. This is where the random walks come into play. We can think of each walk produced in the previous step as a context or word window in a text. Thus, we can maximize the similarities of embeddings of nodes that occur on the same walks.\newline \newline
“In this sense, node sequences in networks correspond to word sequences in text.”

To learn the embeddings via Skip-gram, we first generate random vectors of dimension d for each node. Secondly, we iterate over the set of random walks and update the node embeddings by gradient descent to maximize the probability of the neighbors of a node, given the node itself. This can be achieved by the softmax function. When all of the walks are processed, we can either continue optimization with additional passes over the same walk set or generate new walks on the network.

DeepWalk Algorithm

From above two approaches, we can know that the DeepWalk algorothm can use language modeling approaches to learn node embeddings via leveraging the local structures in the network. With node embeddings at hand, we can use appropriate ML methods for general tasks such as node classification, link prediction, and community detection tasks.
We can see the concrete steps of DeepWalk in following figure.

The random walk generator firstly takes a graph G and samples uniformly a random vertex vi as the root of the random walk Wvi. A walk samples uniformly from the neighbors of the last vertex visited until the maximum length (t) is reached. After generating node-sequences, we need to feed them to a skip-gram model to get node embedding vectors. This entire process is how DeepWalk algorithm works.

By combining both truncated random walks and neural language models we formulate a method which satisfies all of our desired properties. This method generates representations of social networks that are low-dimensional, and exist in a continuous vector space. Its representations encode latent forms of community membership, and because the method outputs useful intermediate representations, it can adapt to changing network topology.

DeepWalk takes a graph as input and produces a latent representation as an output. The result of applying DeepWalk to an undirected graph is shown in above figure. The graph, as typically presented by force-directed layouts, is shown in left figure. Right figure shows the output of our method with 2 latent dimensions. Beyond the striking similarity, we note that linearly separable portions of right figure correspond to clusters found through modularity maximization in the input graph left figure. We can say that it learns a latent space representation of social interactions in Rd. The learned representation encodes community structure so it can be easily exploited by standard classification methods. Note the correspondence between community structure in the left input graph and the right embedding representation. Vertex colors represent a modularity-based clustering of the input graph.

entry.title: Bagging - Random Forest
entry.content:
Bagging

The input is the sample set 𝐷={(𝑥,𝑦1),(𝑥2,𝑦2),…(𝑥𝑚,𝑦𝑚)}, weak learner algorithm 𝐺𝑡(𝑥), weak classifier iteration number T.

The output is the final strong classifier 𝑓(𝑥)
1) For t=1,2…,T:

  • a) Randomly sampling the training set for the t-th time, collecting m times in total, and obtaining a sampling set containing m samples 𝐷𝑡.

  • b) Use the sample set 𝐷𝑡 to train the t-th weak learner 𝐺𝑡(𝑥).

2) If it is a classification algorithm prediction, the category or one of the categories with the most votes cast by T weak learners is the final category. If it is a regression algorithm, the arithmetic average of the regression results obtained by T weak learners is the final model output.


Random Forest

First of all, RF uses the CART decision tree as a weak learner, which reminds us of the gradient boosting tree GBDT.

Second, based on the use of decision trees, RF has made improvements to the establishment of decision trees. For ordinary decision trees, we will select an optimal feature from all n sample features on the node to make the decision tree and the left and right subtrees are divided, but RF randomly selects a part of the sample features on the node, this number is less than n, suppose it is Subtree division. This further enhances the generalization ability of the model.　　　　

If , then the RF CART decision tree is no different from the ordinary CART decision tree. The smaller the , the more robust the model. Of course, the fit to the training set will be worse at this time. That is to say, the smaller the is, the variance of the model will decrease, but the bias will increase. In actual cases, a suitable value is generally obtained through cross-validation tuning.

Except for the above two points, RF is no different from ordinary bagging algorithms.

The following briefly summarizes the RF algorithm.

The input is the sample set 𝐷={(𝑥,𝑦1),(𝑥2,𝑦2),…(𝑥𝑚,𝑦𝑚)}, the number of weak classifier iterations is T.

The output is the final strong classifier 𝑓(𝑥).

1) For t=1,2…,T:

  • a) Randomly sampling the training set for the t-th time, collecting m times in total, and obtaining a sampling set containing m samples 𝐷𝑡

  • b) Use the sampling set 𝐷𝑡 to train the t-th decision tree model 𝐺𝑡(𝑥). When training the node of the decision tree model, select a part of the sample features from all the sample features on the node, and among these randomly selected part of the sample features Choose an optimal feature to divide the left and right subtrees of the decision tree

2) If it is a classification algorithm prediction, the category or one of the categories with the most votes cast by T weak learners is the final category. If it is a regression algorithm, the arithmetic average of the regression results obtained by T weak learners is the final model output.


Advantages of RF

1) Training can be highly parallelized, which has advantages for the training speed of large samples in the era of big data. The main advantage.

2) Since the decision tree nodes can be randomly selected to divide the features, the model can still be trained efficiently when the sample feature dimension is high.

3) After training, the importance of each feature to the output can be given

4) Due to the use of random sampling, the trained model has small variance and strong generalization ability.

5) Compared with Adaboost and GBDT of the Boosting series, RF implementation is relatively simple.

6) Not sensitive to missing some features.
　　

Disadvantages of RF

1) On some sample sets with relatively large noise, the RF model is prone to overfitting.

2) Features with more value divisions are likely to have a greater impact on RF decision-making, thereby affecting the effect of the fitted model.

entry.title: BERT / XLNet / RoBERTa / DistillBERT / Albert
entry.content:
RoBERTa

  1. In BERT, a 15% random mask is applied to sentences, which is a static mask. RoBERTa uses dynamic mask which samples the training corpus 10 times, and then uses 10 different masks for each sample.
  2. The experiment found that removing the NSP pre-trained task has a slight improvement in downstream tasks, so the NSP task is removed in RoBERTa.
  3. More training corpus and larger training batch.

ALBERT

The bert model is too large, and it will bring great challenges to online tasks with high speed and memory requirements. Albert uses some tricks to compress the parameters of BERT, which makes the model smaller.

  • Factorized Embedding Parameterization
    We know that a large number of parameters of the model are due to token embeddings. If the vocab is large, the model parameters will increase a lot. Assuming that V represents the total number of tokens and H represents the embeddings dimension, the entire token embeddings parameters that need to be learned are , and this matrix can be decomposed by two small matrices. , then the parameters from to , because , entire parameter is reduced a lot.

  • Cross-layer parameter sharing
    We know that bert is composed of 12 layers of transformers. Each layer of transformer has its own parameters. The entire parameter amount is relatively large. Albert shares all the layers of transformers with the first layer of transformer parameters, so the whole transformer parameters of ALBERT reduced by 12 times than BERT, and there is only one layer of transformer parameters.

  • Change NSP to SOP
    Change NSP task to sentence-order prediction (SOP) task, which means that the two adjacent sentences in the article are positive samples, and the order of the two sentences is reversed as negative samples

DistilBERT

Knowledge distillation is to use small BERT model structure (student) to learn predictive ability of some big BERT model (teacher). And using some learning strategies to obtain a smaller, faster, but have equivanlent predictive effect to a large model.

Change point:

  1. The DistilBert model and the Bert model have the same structure, but the number of layers is reduced by half, with only 6 layers of transformer structure

  2. When training, let the distilBert model try to learn the effect of the original bert model. And the design of Loss consists of three parts, two of which are related to the output of the bert model.

    • First: Use KL divergence to measure the probability distribution of the final output layer of bert and distilBert (to ensure that the probability distribution of each label predicted by the two models is as consistent as possible)

    • Second: The cos loss of the final output vector of bert and distilBert (to ensure that the output vector representation from the model is as similar as possible), represented by Lcos. The last part is the standard cross entropy, the cross entropy with the real label, represented by Lmlm. The final loss is obtained by adding three parts of weighted loss:

XLNet

Auto Regressive model. The left-to-right language model task can only predict the next word based on the Context-before, or conversely Context-after.

  • Change point:
    Permutation Language Modeling instead of MLM in BERT.

  • Reason:

    • When predicting words, masked words are independent of each other and can not take relationship of Masked words into consideration when doing prediction.
    • BERT pre-trained and fine-tune are not consistent for Masked words. (No mask fine-tune)

Ref

entry.title: BERT / ELMo / GPT
entry.content:
BERT

Overview of BERT

The two steps of how BERT is developed. We can download the model pre-trained in step 1 (trained on un-annotated data), and only worry about fine-tuning it for step 2.

BERT Types

BERT is basically a trained Transformer Encoder stack. The paper provides us 2 types of BERT:
• BERT BASE – L=12, Hidden units=768, heads=12, parameters:110M; Comparable in size to the OpenAI Transformer in order to compare performance
• BERT LARGE – L=24, Hidden units=1024, heads=16, parameters340M; A ridiculously huge model which achieved the state of the art results reported in the paper

How BERT works

BERT makes use of Transformer, an attention mechanism that learns contextual relations between words (or sub-words) in a text. Since BERT’s goal is to generate a language model, only the encoder mechanism is necessary.

As opposed to directional models, which read the text input sequentially (left-to-right or right-to-left), the Transformer encoder reads the entire sequence of words at once. Therefore it is considered bidirectional, though it would be more accurate to say that it’s non-directional. This characteristic allows the model to learn the context of a word based on all of its surroundings (left and right of the word).

BERT uses two training strategies to train the language model:

  • Masked LM (MLM)

    Before feeding word sequences into BERT, 15% of the words in each sequence are replaced with a [MASK] token. The model then attempts to predict the original value of the masked words, based on the context provided by the other, non-masked, words in the sequence. In technical terms, the prediction of the output words requires:
  1. Adding a classification layer on top of the encoder output.
  2. Multiplying the output vectors by the embedding matrix, transforming them into the vocabulary dimension.

  3. Calculating the probability of each word in the vocabulary with softmax.

    The BERT loss function takes into consideration only the prediction of the masked values and ignores the prediction of the non-masked words. As a consequence, the model converges slower than directional models, a characteristic which is offset by its increased context awareness.
    Note: In practice, the BERT implementation is slightly more elaborate and doesn’t replace all of the 15% masked words.

  • Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)

    In the BERT training process, the model receives pairs of sentences as input and learns to predict if the second sentence in the pair is the subsequent sentence in the original document. During training, 50% of the inputs are a pair in which the second sentence is the subsequent sentence in the original document, while in the other 50% a random sentence from the corpus is chosen as the second sentence. The assumption is that the random sentence will be disconnected from the first sentence.

    To help the model distinguish between the two sentences in training, the input is processed in the following way before entering the model:

  1. A [CLS] token is inserted at the beginning of the first sentence and a [SEP] token is inserted at the end of each sentence.
  2. A sentence embedding indicating Sentence A or Sentence B is added to each token. Sentence embeddings are similar in concept to token embeddings with a vocabulary of 2.

  3. A positional embedding is added to each token to indicate its position in the sequence. The concept and implementation of positional embedding are presented in the Transformer paper.

    To predict if the second sentence is indeed connected to the first, the following steps are performed:

    1. The entire input sequence goes through the Transformer model.
    2. The output of the [CLS] token is transformed into a 2×1 shaped vector, using a simple classification layer (learned matrices of weights and biases).
    3. Calculating the probability of IsNextSequence with softmax.

When training the BERT model, Masked LM and Next Sentence Prediction are trained together, with the goal of minimizing the combined loss function of the two strategies.

How to use BERT (Fine-tuning)

Using BERT for a specific task is relatively straightforward:
BERT can be used for a wide variety of language tasks, while only adding a small layer to the core model:

  1. Classification tasks such as sentiment analysis are done similarly to Next Sentence classification, by adding a classification layer on top of the Transformer output for the [CLS] token.
  2. In Question Answering tasks (e.g. SQuAD v1.1), the software receives a question regarding a text sequence and is required to mark the answer in the sequence. Using BERT, a Q&A model can be trained by learning two extra vectors that mark the beginning and the end of the answer.
  3. In Named Entity Recognition (NER), the software receives a text sequence and is required to mark the various types of entities (Person, Organization, Date, etc) that appear in the text. Using BERT, a NER model can be trained by feeding the output vector of each token into a classification layer that predicts the NER label.

Also, we can fine tune the BERT pre-trained model on our own corpus which will make BERT model more suitable for our tasks and data, thereby imporve the performance better.

Ref

ELMo

ELMO is short for Embeddings from Language Model, ELMO is an RNN-based language model whose task is to learn what the next word or previous word in a sentence is.

It is a bo-directional RNN, so that each word corresponds to two different hidden states, and the embedding representation of the word can be obtained by concatenation. When the context of the same word is different, the embedding is different.

With such a multi-layered RNNs, the internal output of each layer is a representation of the word, so which layer of output do we take to represent the embedding of the word? ELMO’s approach is that it need all.

In ELMO, a word will get multiple embeddings, and the weighted summation of different embeddings can get the final embedding for downstream tasks. To illustrate the number of embedding here, the figure below only draws the hidden state of the two-layer RNN output. In fact, the original embedding input to the RNN is also needed, so you will see that the picture in the lower right corner contains three embedding. But different weights are learned based on downstream tasks. There are 5 different tasks in the lower right corner of the above figure, and the embedding weights obtained are different.

  • Difference ELMo / BERT:
    • ELMo use LSTM as feature extractor, while BERT use transformer.

GPT (Generative Pre-Training)

GPT is an Auto-regression model. It use Transformer Decoder side.
The GPT pre-trained tasks are: txt prediction与Task classifier。

  • GPT has a two-stage process
    The first stage is to use the language model for pre-training,
    The second stage solves downstream tasks through the Fine-tuning model.

  • The picture above shows pre-training of GPT, which is similar to ELMO, with two differences (Pre_trained ELMo vs GPT):
    • First of all, the feature extractor is not RNN, but Transformer. As mentioned above, its feature extraction ability is stronger than RNN. This choice is obviously very wise.
    • Secondly, although the pre-training of GPT still takes the language model as the target task, it uses a single directional language model. The so-called “single directional” means that the task of language model training aims to predict words based on the contextual word $W_i$. The words before $W_i$ is called Context-before, and the words after it is called Context-after. ELMO uses both Context-before and Context-after at the same time when doing language model pre-training for predicting the word $W_i$, while GPT only uses the Context-before to make predictions without Context-after.

    Unlike ELMo, which only provides word vectors, GPT pre-trained models are also provided to downstream tasks. The vectors of the model here can be adjusted slightly with new downstream tasks, that is called, fine-tune

entry.title: Do NLP Models Know Numbers? Probing Numeracy in Embeddings
Learning Loss for Active Learning
SummaRuNNer - RNN Based Sequence Model for Extractive Summarization
Recursive Visual Attention in Visual Dialog
Beam Search
entry.content:
Beam Search

An approximate search strategy to choose better results from all possible candidates.

Beam Search algorithm has a parameter called B, which is called the beam width. Let’s see how beam search works by using beam width of 3 in machine translation tasks.

With beam width 3, beam search will consider three words to choose from for the first word. So, in order to perform this first step of Beam search, we run the input French sentence through the encoder network and then the first in the decoder network is a softmax output with overall 10,000 possibilities and we will store the top 3 words because of beam width 3.

Next, for each of the three choices, it will calculate the probability of second word given the first word and the input french sentence. As output of this step, we want to find the pair of the first and second words that is most likely. By the rules of conditional probability, this can be expressed as P of the first words times probability of the second word.

So for this second step of beam search since we have 10,000 words in our vocabulary, we would end up considering three times 10000 or thirty thousand possibilities and then pick the top three.

Because of beam width is equal to three, at every step, we instantiate three copies of the network to evaluate partial sentence fragments and the output. So we will use three copies of the network with different choices for the first words, but these three copies of the network can be very efficiently used to evaluate all 30,000 options for the second word.

And the outcome of this process will be that adding one word at a time until Beam search will decide EOS as the best next symbol.
Notice, if the beam width was 1, then this essentially becomes the greedy search algorithm.

Ref

entry.title: Self Attention
entry.content:
Difference between Self Attention and classic Attention

Self Attention is very different from the traditional Attention mechanism: The traditional Attention calculates Attention based on the hidden state of the source and target, and the result is the dependence relation between each word on the source and each word on the target. However self Attention is different. It only happens on the source side and the target side respectively. Only the Self Attention related to the source input or the target input itself captures the dependency relations between the words on the source side or the target side; and then the self attention obtained in the source side is added to the self attention obtained on the target end to capture the dependency relation between the words on the source end and the target end. Therefore, self attention is better than the traditional attention mechanism. One of the main reasons is that the traditional attention mechanism ignores the dependency between words in the source or target sentence, respectively. In contrast, self Attention can not only get the dependence relationship between the words and words of the source and target ends, and at the same time, the dependence relationship between the words and words of the source or target end can be effectively obtained.

Structure of Self-Attention

Following figure is the structure of self-attention:

For self-attention, the three matrices Q(Query), K(Key), and V(Value) all come from the same input. Firstly, we need to calculate the dot product between Q and K, and then in order to avoid the result being too large, the result will be divided by a scale $\sqrt{d_k}$ , where $d_k$ is the dimension of the query and key vectors. Then use the Softmax operation to normalize the result into a probability distribution, and then multiply it by the value matrix V to get the weighted summation. This operation can be expressed as:

Concrete steps of Self-Attention

We can use a more descriptive example to show the concrete steps of self-attention: assuming we need to translate the phrase: “Thinking Machines”, and we use embedding vector $x_1$ to represent word “Thinking” and embedding vector $x_2$ to represent word “Machines”.
Then:

  • First step: to create three vectors from each of the encoder’s input vectors (in this case, the embedding of each word).
  • Second step: to calculate a scaled score and softmax it. Say we’re calculating the self-attention for the first word in this example, “Thinking”.
  • Third step: to multiply each value vector by the softmax score and then sum up the weighted value vectors.

  • Matrix Calculation of Self-Attention
    To calculate the Query, Key, and Value matrices. We do that by packing our embeddings into a matrix X, and multiplying it by the weight matrices we’ve trained $(W_Q, W_K, W_V)$.

  • Lastly
    since we’re dealing with matrices, we can condense steps in one formula to calculate the outputs of the self-attention layer.

Multi-Head Attention

  • Improvements:
    • It expands the model’s ability to focus on different positions.
    • It gives the attention layer multiple “representation subspaces”.

  • If we do the same self-attention calculation we outlined above, just eight different times with different weight matrices, we end up with eight different Z matrices.

  • This leaves us with a bit of a challenge. The feed-forward layer is not expecting eight matrices – it’s expecting a single matrix (a vector for each word). So we need a way to condense these eight down into a single matrix.
    How do we do that? We concat the matrices then multiple them by an additional weights matrix $W_O$.

  • Here we could see the overall steps of Multi-Head Attention:

Ref

entry.title: Attention in seq2seq
entry.content:
Limitation of classic Encoder-Decoder

  • Compress all the information of the input X to a fixed-length hidden vector Z, ignoring the length of the input X. When the input sentence length is very long, especially when the length of inference sentences is longer than the length of the sentence in the training set, the performance of the model is sharp decline.

  • Encode the input X into a fixed length, and assign the same weight to each word in the sentence. This is unreasonable. For example in machine translation, there is often one input between the input sentence and the output sentence or several words correspond to one or several words of output. Therefore, there is no discrimination for assigning the same weight to each input word, and the performance of the model is often reduced.

Types of Attention

  • Soft Attention
    Traditional attention, which can be embedded in the model for training and gradient propagation

  • Hard Attention
    Hard Attention is a random process model. Hard Attention will not select the output of the entire encoder as its input. Hard Attention will sample the hidden state of the input according to the probability for calculation, rather than the hidden state of the entire Encoder. In order to achieve the back propagation of the gradient, then Monte Carlo sampling method is used to estimate the gradient of the module

  • Global attention
    Same as the traditional Attention model. All hidden states are used to calculate the weight of the context vector.

  • Local attention
    One obvious disadvantage of Global Attention is that all hidden states on the encoder side must participate in the calculation. The calculation overhead will be relatively large, especially when the encoder sentence is too long, for example, a paragraph or an article, the efficiency is partial low. Therefore, in order to improve efficiency, we use Local Attention. Local Attention is an Attention method between Soft Attention and Hard Attention proposed by Kelvin Xu, which combines these two methods.

  • Self Attention
    Self Attention is very different from the traditional Attention mechanism: The traditional Attention calculates Attention based on the hidden state of the source and target, and the result is the dependence relation between each word on the source and each word on the target. However self Attention is different. It only happens on the source side and the target side respectively. Only the Self Attention related to the source input or the target input itself captures the dependency relations between the words on the source side or the target side; and then the self attention obtained in the source side is added to the self attention obtained on the target end to capture the dependency relation between the words on the source end and the target end. Therefore, self attention is better than the traditional attention mechanism. One of the main reasons is that the traditional attention mechanism ignores the dependency between words in the source or target sentence, respectively. In contrast, self Attention can not only get the dependence relationship between the words and words of the source and target ends, and at the same time, the dependence relationship between the words and words of the source or target end can be effectively obtained.

Soft Attention details

  • Drawback of normal encoder-decoder mechanism: The only connection between Encoder and Decoder is intermediate vector $C$, which encodes the information of the entire input sequence into a fixed-size state vector and then decodes it, which can be seen as “loss compression” of the information. So the output sequence relies heavily on the context defined by the hidden state in the final output of the encoder, making it challenging for the model to deal with long sentences. Also the intermediate semantic vector cannot fully express the information of the entire input sequence. And as the length of the input information increases, the length of the vector is fixed, the previously encoded information will be overwritten by the later information, and a lot of information will be lost. In the case of long sequences, there is a high probability that the initial context has been lost by the end of the sequence.

  • Solution: Bahdanau et al., 2014 and Luong et al., 2015 papers introduced and a technique called “Attention” which allows the model to focus on different parts of the input sequence at every stage of the output sequence allowing the context to be preserved from beginning to end.
    Therefore we can realize that Attention is the mechanism that forces the model to learn to focus (=to attend) on specific parts of the input sequence when decoding, instead of relying only on the hidden vector of the decoder’s RNNs. So here is the new representation.

  • Encoder calculation figure:

  • The input is ‘Cat, chase, mouse’, the hidden state in encoder is $h_1, h_2, h_3$, can be seen as the represention of words: Cat, chase, mouse.

  • Use to represent the correlation (attention) between in the j-th stage of Encoder and the i-th stage in Decoder, and calculate the intermediate semantic vector required for decoder. has the closest relationship with “cat”, and the corresponding is larger than and ; while has the closest relationship with “cat”, the corresponding is larger than and ; in the same way, has the closest relationship with “mouse” and is similar The corresponding is larger than and .

  • How to calculate Attention $a_{ij}$
    The j-th hidden layer unit in the Encoder and the i-th hidden layer unit of the Decoder are calculated to obtain .
    Following is how we could calculate the , hidden state of decoder , :

entry.title: Seq2seq - Encoder - Decoder
entry.content:
What is Seq2seq model?

Sequence-to-sequence (Seq2Seq) models are deep learning models that have achieved a lot of success in tasks like machine translation, text summarization, and image captioning.

And what is the Seq2Seq Model? More specifically, Seq2Seq model is a model that takes a sequence of items (words, letters, time series, etc) as inputs and outputs another sequence of items.

In the case of Neural Machine Translation, the input is a series of words, and the output is the translated series of words.

And the internal seq2seq model is composed of an encoder and a decoder. The encoder captures the context of the input sequence in the form of a hidden state vector and sends it to the decoder, which then produces the output sequence. Since the task is sequence based, both the encoder and decoder tend to use some form of RNNs, LSTMs, GRUs, etc.

We could say:

Encoder + Decoder = Seq2seq



Encoder-Decoder Architecture

The Seq2Seq framework relies on the encoder-decoder paradigm. The encoder encodes the input sequence, while the decoder produces the target sequence.

The encoder-decoder architecture is a neural network design pattern. As shown in Figure, the architecture is partitioned into two parts, the encoder and the decoder. The encoder’s role is to encode the inputs into state, which often contains several tensors. Then the state is passed into the decoder to generate the outputs.

  • Encoder

    Our input sequence is “how are you”. Each word from the input sequence is associated to a vector $wR$ (via a lookup table). In our case, we have 3 words, thus our input will be transformed into R. Then, we simply run an LSTM over this sequence of vectors and store the last hidden state outputed by the LSTM: this will be our encoder representation $e$ vector.

  • Decoder

    Now that we have a vector $e$ that captures the meaning of the input sequence, we’ll use it to generate the target sequence word by word. Feed to another NN cell, normally we use LSTM or GRU. Like the following figure.

  • Normally we have 2 different types of Encoder-Decoder architecture:

entry.title: Transformer
entry.content:
Architecture of Transformer

At first, we can see the overall architecture of Transformer below:

The Encoder side

The encoding component is a stack of encoders (the paper stacks six of them on top of each other – there’s nothing magical about the number six, one can definitely experiment with other arrangements). The decoding component is a stack of decoders of the same number.

The encoders are all identical in structure (yet they do not share weights). Each one is broken down into two sub-layers。
The encoder’s inputs first flow through a self-attention layer – a layer that helps the encoder look at other words in the input sentence as it encodes a specific word. We’ll look closer at self-attention later in the post.

The outputs of the self-attention layer are fed to a feed-forward neural network. The exact same feed-forward network is independently applied to each position.

The decoder has both those layers, but between them is an attention layer that helps the decoder focus on relevant parts of the input sentence (similar what attention does in seq2seq models).

For the encoder, there are two layers, a self-attention layer and a feedforward neural network layer. Self-attention can help the current state not only pay attention to the current word, so as to obtain the semantics of the context.

The decoder also contains the two-layer network mentioned by the encoder, but there is also an attention layer between the two layers to help the current state get the current key content that needs to be paid attention to.

After the end of Enmbedding, input embedding result to the encoder layer. After processing of self-attention, the data is sent to the feedforward neural network. And the calculation of the feedforward neural network can be parallelized, and the obtained output will be input to the next encoder.

  • Positional Embedding

    The transformer adds a vector to each input embedding. These vectors follow a specific pattern that the model learns, which helps it determine the position of each word, or the distance between different words in the sequence. The intuition here is that adding these values to the embeddings provides meaningful distances between the embedding vectors once they’re projected into Q/K/V vectors and during dot-product attention.

    Following formular is how to calculate positional embedding vectors.
    pos is the position of current word, i is the index for each value of vector. We can find that, we use sin in the even position, and cos in the odd position.

  • Layer Normalization

    In the transformer, each sub-layer (self-attetion, ffnn) will be followed by a residual module, and there will also be a Layer normalization.

    This goes for the sub-layers of the decoder as well. If we’re to think of a Transformer of 2 stacked encoders and decoders, it would look something like this:

The Decoder side

Now that we’ve covered most of the concepts on the encoder side, we basically know how the components of decoders work as well. But let’s take a look at how they work together.

The encoder start by processing the input sequence. The output of the top encoder is then transformed into a set of attention vectors K and V. These are to be used by each decoder in its “encoder-decoder attention” layer which helps the decoder focus on appropriate places in the input sequence

We can find that the decoder side is actually the same as the encoder side, but there is an additional masked mutil-head attetion at the bottom of Decoder. The mask here is also a key technology of the transformer. Let’s take a look below.

Mask

Mask represents a mask, which masks certain values so that it has no effect when the parameters are updated. There are two types of masks involved in the Transformer model: padding mask and sequence mask.
Among them, the padding mask needs to be used in all scaled dot-product attention, and the sequence mask is only used in the self-attention of the decoder.

  • Padding Mask
    What is a padding mask? Because the length of each batch of input sequence is different, which means, we have to align the input sequence. Specifically, we have to fill 0 after the shorter sequence while the content on the left is intercepted if the input sequence is too long and the excess is discarded directly. Because these filling positions are actually meaningless, our attention mechanism should not focus on these positions, so we need to do some processing.

    The specific method is to add a very large negative number (negative infinity) to the value of these positions. In this case, the probability of these positions will be close to 0 after softmax. And our padding mask is actually a tensor, each value is a Boolean, the value of false is where we want to process.

  • Sequence mask
    The sequence mask is to make the decoder unable to see future information. For instance, for a sequence at the moment time_step is t, our decoding output should only depend on the output before time t, and not on the output after t. Therefore, we need to find a way to hide the information after t.

    So how can we do it? It’s also very simple: generate an upper triangular matrix, the values of the upper triangle are all set to 0. Apply this matrix to each sequence to achieve our goal.

    For the self-attention of the decoder, the scaled dot-product attention used in it also requires padding mask and sequence mask as attn_mask. The specific implementation is to add two masks as “attn_mask”. In other cases, “attn_mask” is equal to padding mask.

  • Difference on mask-self-attention and encoder-decoder-attention
    Regarding these two attention layers in Decoder, it can be understood that: mask-self-attention aims to calculate the relationship between the currently translated content and the previous text that has been translated, while encoder-decoder-attention aims to calculate the relationship between current translation content and the feature vectors from Encoder.
  • Decoder input and output
    • Output: the probability distribution of the output word corresponding to position i
    • Input: the output of the encoder & the output of the decoder at i-1 position. So the intermediate encoder-decoder-attention is not a self-attention, its K and V come from the encoder, and Q comes from the output of the previous state in Decoder.
    • Decoder: Special attention should be paid here. The encoder can be calculated in parallel and all encoding is done at one time, but decoder is not solved on all sequences at once, but solved one by one like rnn, because the Query is used as attention of input at the previous position of Decoder.

Output side

When the decoder is all executed, how to map the obtained vector to the words we need? We just add a fully connected layer and a softmax layer at the end.

entry.title: DevBot
entry.content:
Overview (Function) of DevBot

  • Translate natural language query to Pandas code
  • Recommend next step data manipulations based on user historic processing

Intent & Entity resolution

The DevBot can resolve the actual intent that user want to do which manipulations on data, also extract the entities as the required parameters in data manipulation functions.


Next data manipulation recommendation

DevBot use two different approaches for data manipulation recommendation. One is rule-based approach which recommend data manipulations based on user historic queries, which another approach apply NLG seq2seq model for next manipoulation generation.


More Interactive

The DevBot can provide us more interactive responses for asking us required or necessary entities as parameters.


Embeded as VS Code extention



Embeded as Python package PyPI

entry.title: How to develop Python Package as PyPI
entry.content:
Why developing own Package

There are a bunch of nifty tools that help you build, install and distribute packages.

Using a well structured, standard layout for our own package makes it easy to use those tools.

Even if you never want to give anyone else your code, a well structured package simplifies development.

What is a Package?

A collection of modules:
… and the documentation
… and the tests
… and any top-level scripts
… and any data files required
… and a way to build and install it…

Steps to develop Python Package

1. We should install the necessary packages for developing our own python package:

pip install setuptools
pip install twine

2. We should construct the required structure for python package as follow:

Firstly we should build a new folder named: OurPyPI. Then the file we should build in this folder:

3. Then we should write content in file setup.py
4. We should define our own package functions

In folder OurPyPI/main.py file, we can add and define the functions or class we need in the package.

For example, we define a function in file OurPyPI/main.py:

5. Package and Publish our own Python Package on PyPI

  • run ‘python setup.py check’, continue if no error reported.

  • run ‘python3 setup.py sdist bdist_wheel’ to package all files.

  • run ‘twine upload dist/*’ to publish our own developing package into Python Package PyPI. Before this step, we should register an account in PyPI.

  • Then we can use ‘pip install OurPyPI==.0.1’ to install our own package.

6. Test and usage of our own package

  • How to test our own package locally.
    In the same developing environment and fold OurPyPI, we can use command ‘pip install -e ./OurPyPI/‘ to install the package locally.

  • How to use our own package.

    Then we can get print value ‘Hi, Mike’ in console.
